content.text:
Design a Distributed Messaging System for Service Communication like Kafka, RabbitMQ, etc.
HARD
role
Senior Software Engineer
Round 1
System Design Round
System Design (HLD)
exp
#1556 · Mar 2026
○ no offer
High-Level Design (HLD)
High Scalability
High Availability
Kafka
RabbitMQ
Message Queue
Message Broker

▸
View problem statement & approach
Problem statement
Design a scalable and fault-tolerant messaging system for communication between services, similar to Kafka or RabbitMQ:

Requirements:
Core Functionalities:

Publishers: Services that publish messages to topics or queues.
Subscribers: Services that consume messages from topics or queues.
Messaging Broker: A distributed system that ensures reliable message delivery and decouples publishers from subscribers.
Key Features:

Durability: Messages should persist in case of broker failure.
Scalability: Handle high throughput of messages across multiple publishers and subscribers.
Fault Tolerance: Ensure no message loss and guaranteed delivery even in case of node failures.
Ordering: Maintain message order within topics or partitions.
Delivery Semantics: Support at-least-once, at-most-once, and exactly-once delivery modes.
Extension:

Retention policies (e.g., message expiry).
Handling dead-letter queues for unprocessed messages.

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — durability, ordering, and exactly-once — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: we want a **firehose of throughput** (millions of messages/sec) AND **zero message loss** AND **ordering** AND **flexible delivery guarantees** — and those pull against each other. Durability and ordering want us to write everything to one ordered log on disk; throughput wants us to spread writes across many machines. The whole design is about resolving that with **partitioning + replication**.

🏢 **Company x Level lens:** I'll target a **Google-style distributed-systems bar** — I'll go deepest on one hard problem (the replication/consistency core) and name rejected alternatives. Tell me if this is Amazon (I'll make cost & ops first-class) or a startup (I'll start with a managed broker) and I'll re-tilt. This is a **senior** round so I'll build one coherent system with real depth rather than an org-wide platform.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Publishers can publish a message to a named topic** (optionally to a specific partition via a key), and get an ack.
- **Subscribers can consume messages from a topic**, in order within a partition, tracking their position (offset).
- **The broker durably stores and replicates messages** so nothing is lost when a node dies, and delivers them reliably.
- **Support configurable delivery semantics** — at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once.
- **Consumer groups** — multiple consumers share a topic's partitions so we can scale out consumption.

**Below the line (out of scope for now):** retention policies & TTL expiry, dead-letter queues, schema registry, multi-tenancy/quotas, geo-replication, ACL/auth admin UI. *(I'll touch retention + DLQ in deep dives as extensions.)*

**Non-Functional (the system should…):**
- **Be highly available** for both publish and consume — target **99.99%** (≈ 52 min/yr down); a node failure must not lose acked data.
- **Be durable** — an acked message survives f node failures (replication factor giving us that). **RPO ≈ 0** for acked, fully-replicated writes.
- **Be high throughput / low latency** — handle **~1M msgs/sec** sustained; **p99 publish ack < 10ms** (in-region), consume add-latency single-digit ms.
- **Preserve ordering within a partition** (not globally — that's the deliberate trade-off).
- **Scale horizontally** — add brokers/partitions to grow linearly.
- **Consistency:** strong/linearizable for the *committed offset* of a partition (one source of truth per partition); consumer offset commits are durable.

> 💬 "The thing I want to call out up front is that global ordering across a whole topic is incompatible with high throughput — so I'll give strong ordering *per partition* and make partitioning a first-class design decision."

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true **exactly-once** end-to-end is only achievable with idempotent producers + transactional consumers; I'll be precise that the broker gives exactly-once *within the system*, and end-to-end requires the consumer to participate.

🎙️ **Script:** "So we're building a distributed pub/sub log like Kafka. Publishers write messages to topics, subscribers read them, and the broker in the middle durably stores and replicates everything so we never lose an acked message even if a machine dies. The hard requirements are durability, ordering, and flexible delivery guarantees — at-most, at-least, and exactly-once. My one big up-front decision is that I'll guarantee ordering *per partition*, not globally, because global ordering would bottleneck the whole topic on a single machine. I'm targeting four nines of availability, about a million messages a second, and single-digit-millisecond publish acks."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each changes the design:
- **"What's the message size and throughput profile?"** — Small (a few KB) high-volume events vs large payloads changes batching, and whether we store payloads inline or in blob storage.
- **"Pub/sub (many independent consumers, like Kafka) or work-queue (compete for messages, like RabbitMQ)?"** — This is the biggest fork. Kafka = a *retained log* multiple groups re-read; RabbitMQ = messages *deleted on ack*. I'll design the **log model (Kafka-style)** and show how a queue is a special case.
- **"How long must messages be retained?"** — Drives storage sizing and tiering (local disk vs object storage).
- **"Which delivery semantic is the priority?"** — Most systems live on at-least-once + idempotent consumers; exactly-once is expensive.
- **"Single region or multi-region?"** — Multi-region forces a story on cross-region replication lag and which region owns writes.
- **"Ordering scope — per key, per partition, or global?"** — Confirms the per-partition trade-off.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** Kafka-style **retained, partitioned, replicated log**; ~1M msgs/sec, avg 1KB; single region, multi-AZ; 7-day retention; at-least-once is the default with exactly-once as an opt-in; ordering is per-partition.

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to design the Kafka-style retained-log model rather than a delete-on-ack work queue, since it generalizes to both. Are you good with that, or do you specifically want RabbitMQ-style competing-consumer queues?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and only compute the few numbers that drive decisions — throughput, storage footprint, and partition count.

print(f"Ingress: {ingress_MBs:.0f} MB/s  ~{ingress_Gbps:.1f} Gbps (1 copy)")

# replication factor 3 -> writes traverse network 2 extra times
repl=3
print(f"Replicated disk write rate: {ingress_MBs*repl:.0f} MB/s across cluster")

# fan-out reads: assume 3 consumer groups each reading full stream
groups=3
egress_MBs=ingress_MBs*groups
print(f"Egress (3 groups): {egress_MBs:.0f} MB/s ~{egress_MBs*8/1000:.1f} Gbps")

# storage 7 day retention, replicated
secs=7*24*3600
raw_TB=msgs_per_sec*avg_bytes*secs/1e12
print(f"Raw 7d: {raw_TB:.0f} TB  x{repl} replicas = {raw_TB*repl:.0f} TB")

# partitions: a single partition leader handles ~ tens of MB/s comfortably, say 10MB/s
part_throughput_MBs=10
parts=ingress_MBs/part_throughput_MBs
print(f"Partitions needed (~10MB/s each): ~{parts:.0f}")

# brokers: a broker ~ 100-200MB/s write nic+disk; with replication factor traffic
broker_MBs=125  # ~1Gbps usable

content.text:
Here's what actually matters, rounded to say-out-loud figures: | Metric | Value | Why it drives a decision | |---|---|---| | Ingress (1 copy) | ~1 GB/s (~8 Gbps) | One machine can't take this → must partition | | Replicated write rate | ~3 GB/s across cluster | Replication factor 3 triples disk+network | | Egress (3 consumer groups) | ~3 GB/s (~25 Gbps) | Fan-out, not ingest, often dominates NIC | | Storage, 7-day, replicated | ~1.9 PB | Forces tiered storage to object store | | **Partitions needed** | **~100** | The number that sets parallelism | | Brokers (~125 MB/s each) | ~25 | Cluster size baseline | **QPS** *(queries/requests per second — here messages/sec)*. **The one number that forces the design: ~100 partitions.** A single partition is an ordered log owned by one leader broker, and a leader comfortably does ~10 MB/s with replication; at 1 GB/s ingress we *must* spread across ~100 partitions. **Flip threshold:** below ~10 MB/s a single partition (one machine, perfect global ordering) is fine — above it, partitioning is mandatory and global ordering dies. 🎙️ **Script:** "Let me size the few numbers that matter. We're ingesting about a gigabyte a second, which with three-way replication means roughly three gigabytes a second of disk writes across the cluster — and if three consumer groups each read the full stream, egress is another three gig a second, so fan-out actually dominates my network. Seven days of retention replicated is almost two petabytes, which tells me I'll tier cold data to object storage. The headline number is partitions: a single ordered log on one machine tops out around ten megabytes a second, so to hit a gigabyte a second I need about a hundred partitions spread over roughly twenty-five brokers. That partition count is the spine of everything that follows." ## 4. Core Entities First draft of the key nouns — I'll detail fields in the data model later: - **Topic** — a named logical stream of messages (e.g. `payments`). A category publishers write to. - **Partition** — an ordered, append-only log; a topic is split into N partitions. **The unit of ordering, parallelism, and replication.** - **Message / Record** — key + value + headers + timestamp, assigned a monotonic **offset** within its partition. - **Broker** — a server that hosts partition replicas (some as leader, some as follower). - **Producer** — a client that publishes; chooses partition via key hash or round-robin. - **Consumer & Consumer Group** — clients that read; a group divides partitions among its members and tracks committed **offsets**. - **Offset** — a consumer's position (the cursor) in a partition. - **Controller** — the cluster coordinator that assigns leaders and tracks membership. 🎙️ **Script:** "The key nouns are topics, which are split into partitions — and the partition is the star: it's the unit of ordering, parallelism, and replication all at once. Each message gets a monotonic offset inside its partition. Brokers host partition replicas, producers write, consumer groups read and remember their offset, and a controller coordinates who's leader. I'll keep fields light for now and flesh them out in the data model." ## 5. API / Interface Default to a **binary TCP protocol** (not REST) — at 1M msgs/sec we need batching, long-lived connections, and zero-copy; HTTP per-message overhead would dominate. I'll describe it in REST-ish terms for clarity. **Security:** clients authenticate via mTLS/SASL; the broker derives identity from the connection and checks topic ACLs — never trusts a client-supplied producer id for authorization. **Produce** (serves "publish a message"): ``` PRODUCE topic, [ {key, value, headers} ... ], // batched acks = 0 | 1 | all, producer_id, producer_epoch, sequence_no // for idempotency/EOS -> { partition, base_offset } per batch | error ``` - `acks=all` = wait for all in-sync replicas (durability); `acks=1` = leader only; `acks=0` = fire-and-forget (at-most-once). The `producer_id + sequence_no` give the broker an idempotency key to dedup retries. **Fetch** (serves "consume in order"): ``` FETCH topic, partition, fetch_offset, max_bytes -> [ records with offsets ], high_watermark ``` - Pull-based (consumer asks). `high_watermark` = highest offset safely replicated = the last offset a consumer is allowed to read (never read un-replicated data). **CommitOffset** (serves offset tracking / at-least vs at-most-once): ``` COMMIT group_id, topic, partition, offset // durable, to __offsets topic FETCH_OFFSET group_id, topic, partition -> last committed offset ``` **Group management:** ``` JOIN_GROUP group_id, member_id -> assigned partitions (rebalance) HEARTBEAT group_id, member_id -> ok | rebalance ``` > 💬 "I'm deliberately not using REST per message — I'd lose to HTTP overhead at a million a second. It's a binary protocol with batching and long-lived connections, and the `acks` knob is how the client picks its durability-vs-latency trade-off." 🎙️ **Script:** "Producers call Produce with a batch of records and an `acks` setting — that one knob chooses durability versus latency. Consumers pull with Fetch from a given offset and only ever see data up to the high-watermark, which is the highest fully-replicated offset. They commit their position with CommitOffset, and committing *before* versus *after* processing is literally what flips at-most-once versus at-least-once. Group management handles dividing partitions across consumers and rebalancing when one dies. It's a binary protocol, not REST, because batching and long-lived sockets are how we survive a million messages a second." ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) ### 6.1 "Publishers can publish a message to a topic" Start minimal: a producer sends a record to a broker; the broker appends it to the partition log on disk and returns the offset. ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Producer] -->|1 PRODUCE batch by key| LB[Partitioner picks partition] LB -->|2 route to leader| B1[(Broker leader
partition P0 log)] B1 -->|3 append to disk| D1[(Segment files)] B1 -->|4 ack base_offset| P classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class P,LB,B1,D1 cold; ``` 1. Producer batches records and picks a partition: `hash(key) % N` (same key → same partition → ordering), or round-robin if no key. 2. Routes to the **leader broker** for that partition (client caches the topic→leader map from metadata). 3. Leader appends to the active **segment file** — a sequential disk write, which is why it's fast (disks are great at sequential I/O). 4. Leader returns the assigned `base_offset`. State change: the log grew by one batch; offset counter advanced. > 💬 "Notice the partition is chosen by hashing the key — that's how I guarantee all messages for, say, one user land in the same partition and stay ordered." ### 6.2 "Subscribers can consume in order, tracking offset" Add consumers and the offset store. Consumers **pull** (so a slow consumer never gets overwhelmed — backpressure for free). ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Producer] -->|1 produce| B1[(Broker leader P0)] B1 --> D1[(Segment files)] C[Consumer group G1] -->|2 FETCH from offset| B1 B1 -->|3 records up to high-watermark| C C -->|4 COMMIT offset| OFF[(__offsets topic)] classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class P,B1,D1 cold; class C,OFF hot; ``` 1. Producer writes (as before). 2. Consumer fetches from its last committed offset. 3. Broker returns records up to the **high-watermark** only. 4. Consumer commits its new offset to the internal `__offsets` topic (itself a durable partitioned log — we eat our own dog food). State change: the group's cursor advances durably. > 💬 "Consumers pull rather than the broker pushing — that gives me natural backpressure and lets each consumer group read at its own pace from the same retained log." ### 6.3 "Durably store & replicate so nothing is lost" Now the core. Each partition has **R replicas** (R=3): one **leader**, R-1 **followers**. Followers continuously fetch from the leader. The set of replicas caught up is the **ISR** (in-sync replicas). A record is **committed** (advances high-watermark) once all ISR have it. A **Controller** assigns leaders and reacts to failures. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Coord[Coordination] CTRL[Controller
Raft quorum] end P[Producer] -->|1 PRODUCE acks=all| L[(Leader P0)] L -->|2 replicate| F1[(Follower P0-b)] L -->|2 replicate| F2[(Follower P0-c)] F1 -->|3 ack| L F2 -->|3 ack| L L -->|4 advance HW + ack| P CTRL -. assigns leader / ISR .-> L CTRL -. promotes on failure .-> F1 C[Consumer] -->|5 FETCH <= HW| L classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class P,L,F1,F2 cold; class C hot; class CTRL hot; ``` 1. Producer sends with `acks=all`. 2. Leader appends locally and followers fetch/replicate it. 3. Followers ack once persisted. 4. Once **all ISR** have it, leader advances high-watermark and acks the producer — *now* it's durable (RPO≈0). 5. Consumers only ever read ≤ high-watermark, so they never see data that could be lost on leader failure. The **Controller** (a small Raft-replicated quorum — Kafka's KRaft, replacing the old ZooKeeper) tracks broker liveness and partition leadership; if the leader dies it promotes an ISR follower. **Kafka** *(the system we're emulating — a distributed append-only commit log)*. **Raft** *(a consensus algorithm where a majority of nodes agree on an ordered log — here it keeps cluster metadata, like who's leader, consistent even during failures)*. **ISR** *(in-sync replicas — the followers fully caught up to the leader; we only ack and only fail over to these, so we never lose committed data)*. > 💬 "The magic is the high-watermark: consumers can only read data that's already on all in-sync replicas, so a leader crash can never expose a message that then vanishes." **Final (high-level):** ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Clients P[Producers] C[Consumer groups] end subgraph Cluster[Broker cluster ~25 nodes] CTRL[Controller
Raft] L0[(P0 leader)] L1[(P1 leader)] R0[(P0 followers x2)] R1[(P1 followers x2)] OFF[(__offsets log)] end COLD[(Object storage
tiered old segments)] P -->|produce by key| L0 P -->|produce by key| L1 L0 --> R0 L1 --> R1 C -->|fetch <= HW| L0 C -->|fetch <= HW| L1 C -->|commit offset| OFF CTRL -. leadership/ISR .-> L0 CTRL -. leadership/ISR .-> L1 L0 -. age out segments .-> COLD classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class P,L0,L1,R0,R1,CTRL,COLD cold; class C,OFF hot; ``` Load-bearing schema on the partition log: `offset (monotonic), key, value, timestamp, leader_epoch`. 🎙️ **Script:** "End to end: producers hash the key to a partition and send to that partition's leader broker. The leader appends to a sequential log on disk, followers replicate it, and only once all in-sync replicas have the message does the leader advance the high-watermark and ack — that's our durability point. Consumers pull from the leader, but only up to that high-watermark, so they never read data that could disappear. They track their own progress with committed offsets stored in an internal log. A small Raft-based controller watches everyone and promotes a follower if a leader dies. Old segments age out to object storage to keep the hot tier cheap." 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton — produce, replicated durable store, ordered consume, and failover. Where do you want me deepest: the replication/exactly-once core, the partitioning & hot-key story, or retention & dead-letter handling?" ## 7. Data Model & Storage | Entity | Key fields | Store | Why | |---|---|---|---| | Partition log | offset, key, value, headers, ts, leader_epoch | **Append-only segment files on local SSD**, tiered to object storage | Sequential writes/reads; the log *is* the database | | Index | offset → file position; ts → offset | **Sparse memory-mapped index per segment** | O(log n) seek to any offset without scanning | | Offsets | group, topic, partition, offset | **Compacted internal topic** `__offsets` | Durable, only latest value per key matters | | Cluster metadata | topic→partitions, partition→leader+ISR, broker liveness | **Raft-replicated KV (controller quorum)** | Must be strongly consistent; small volume | | Consumer group state | members, assignment, generation | **Group coordinator (a broker) + internal topic** | Needs durability across rebalances | **Storage choices glossed:** the partition is an **append-only log** split into **segments** (e.g. 1GB files) — we delete or tier whole segments for retention, which is O(1). A **sparse index** (memory-mapped) maps offsets to byte positions so a fetch jumps straight to the data. **Log compaction** *(keep only the latest value per key)* is what makes the `__offsets` topic bounded. **Partition/shard key = the message key** (`hash(key) % N`); ordering & consistency are **per partition** (one leader = one writer = linearizable per partition), while cluster metadata is **globally strongly consistent** via Raft. 🎙️ **Script:** "The storage trick is that the log *is* the database — append-only segment files on SSD, with a sparse in-memory index so we can seek to any offset fast. Retention is just deleting or tiering whole segment files, which is O(1). Offsets and group state live in internal compacted topics, so the broker stores its own metadata the same way it stores everything else. The only thing needing strong global consistency is cluster metadata — who's leader — and that lives in a small Raft quorum." ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "what does a partition guarantee and where does the high-watermark sit" — almost every answer falls out of that. ### How do we guarantee no message loss with high throughput? (Replication) **#### Bad: single leader, async replication, ack on local write.** **Approach:** leader writes to disk, acks immediately, replicates to followers in the background. **Challenges / ⚠️ Trap:** the leader acks then crashes before followers copy the data → **acked message lost**. This is the classic trap: `acks=1` *feels* safe but silently drops data on failover. Violates RPO≈0. **#### Good: synchronous replication to all R replicas.** **Approach:** ack only after every replica persists. **Challenges:** one slow/dead replica stalls *all* writes — availability tanks the moment any of 3 nodes hiccups. Too brittle. **#### Great: ISR + high-watermark + `acks=all` with `min.insync.replicas`.** **Approach:** maintain the **In-Sync Replica** set dynamically. Ack when all *currently in-sync* replicas have the record, and require at least `min.insync.replicas` (e.g. 2 of 3). A lagging follower is *evicted* from ISR so it doesn't block writes; it rejoins after catching up. On leader failure, only an ISR member can be promoted → no committed data lost. The high-watermark gates what consumers see. ```mermaid flowchart TD L[(Leader P0)] -->|replicate| F1[(Follower in ISR)] L -->|replicate| F2[(Follower lagging)] F2 -.evicted from ISR.-> X[Not counted for ack] L -->|ack when ISR>=min.insync=2| P[Producer] CTRL[Controller] -.promote only ISR member.-> F1 classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class L,F1,F2,X,P,CTRL cold; ``` **Trade-off:** if ISR shrinks below `min.insync.replicas`, we **reject writes** (choose consistency over availability — a CP choice for that partition). That's the right call: better to refuse than to silently lose data. This is exactly what real Kafka does. 🎙️ **Script:** "The trick is the in-sync replica set. We don't wait for *all* replicas — we wait for the ones currently caught up, and we require a minimum of two of three. A slow follower gets kicked out of the set so it can't stall writes, and crucially we only ever promote an in-sync replica to leader, so a committed message can never be lost. If too many replicas fall behind, we'd rather reject the write than lie about durability." 🧠 **If they ask "why not just ack on the leader?":** "Because the leader can die right after acking and before any follower copies the data — that's silent data loss on failover. ISR gives me throughput *and* the guarantee." ### How do we preserve ordering while scaling out? Jump to **Great** — this one's clear. **Ordering is guaranteed only within a partition**, because a partition has exactly one leader (one writer) and a monotonic offset. To keep related messages ordered, **route by key**: `hash(key) % N`. All events for one user/order land in one partition, ordered; unrelated keys parallelize across partitions. **Trap:** assuming you get *global* ordering — you don't, and asking for it serializes the whole topic. Also, **changing partition count reshuffles `hash(key)%N`**, breaking key→partition stability → either over-provision partitions up front or use consistent-hashing-style remapping. And with an idempotent producer you must cap **in-flight batches** appropriately or retries can reorder within a partition. 🎙️ **Script:** "Ordering lives at the partition level because each partition has a single leader and a monotonic offset. I route by key hash so everything for one user stays in one partition and stays ordered, while different keys spread out for throughput. The thing to watch is that adding partitions changes the hash mapping, so I'd over-provision partitions early rather than reshuffle keys later." ### How do we support at-most / at-least / exactly-once? **#### At-most-once (Bad-for-most, fine for metrics):** `acks=0` and commit offset *before* processing. Fast, lossy. Use for telemetry where dropping is OK. **#### At-least-once (Good, the default):** `acks=all`, process the message, *then* commit offset. On crash before commit, you reprocess → duplicates. Requires **idempotent consumers** (dedup by message id). This is what 90% of real systems run. **#### Exactly-once (Great, expensive):** two mechanisms combine: - **Idempotent producer:** each producer gets a `producer_id` + per-partition `sequence_no`; the broker rejects duplicates from retries → no dupes on the write side. - **Transactions:** producing to output topics *and* committing input offsets happen atomically via a transaction coordinator + two-phase commit, with consumers reading at `read_committed` isolation. ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Producer] -->|seq_no per partition| B[(Broker dedup
last seq per producer)] B -->|reject if seq<=last| P TC[Txn coordinator] -. atomic: produce + commit offset .-> B C[Consumer read_committed] --> B classDef cold fill:#3d1f0b,stroke:#d29922,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; classDef hot fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3; class P,B,TC cold; class C hot; ``` **Trade-off:** exactly-once adds latency (txn markers, 2PC) and complexity, and is only *end-to-end* exactly-once if the consumer's side effects are also transactional/idempotent. Most teams choose at-least-once + idempotent consumers as the pragmatic sweet spot. 🎙️ **Script:** "At-most-once is fire-and-forget — fast, lossy, fine for metrics. At-least-once is the workhorse: ack-all, process, then commit, and you dedup on the consumer side. Exactly-once needs two things working together — an idempotent producer that tags each record with a sequence number so the broker drops retries, plus transactions that atomically commit the output and the input offset. It's real but it costs latency, so I default to at-least-once with idempotent consumers unless the use case demands it." 🧠 **If they ask "is exactly-once truly possible?":** "Within the broker's own read-process-write, yes, via idempotent producer plus transactions. End-to-end it requires the consumer's external side effects to also be idempotent or transactional — otherwise you've just moved the dedup problem." ### Hot partition / hot key **Problem:** one key (e.g. a celebrity, a giant tenant) gets disproportionate traffic → its partition's leader is the binding bottleneck (~10 MB/s ceiling) while others idle. **Relief:** (1) **salt the key** (`key#bucket`) to spread one logical key across sub-partitions — costs strict per-key ordering, so only when ordering for that key isn't required; (2) ensure leaders for hot partitions are spread across *different* brokers (leadership balancing); (3) for a hot *consumer*, the partition count caps parallelism — over-provision partitions so a group can scale. **New ceiling:** with salting into 10 buckets, that key now does ~100 MB/s across 10 leaders. 🎙️ **Script:** "If one key is hot, its single partition leader becomes the bottleneck because ordering forces one writer. I'd salt that key across several sub-partitions to spread the load — accepting that I lose strict ordering for just that key — and make sure the hot partitions' leaders sit on different brokers." ### Retention & Dead-Letter Queues (the extensions) **Retention:** each segment is timestamped; a background process deletes (or tiers to object storage) whole segments older than the policy (time-based, e.g. 7 days, or size-based). O(1) per segment — no per-message scanning. **Log compaction** is the alternative for "keep latest value per key" (changelog topics). **Dead-letter queue:** at-least-once means a poison message can be retried forever. The consumer retries N times with backoff; after that it publishes the message (+ failure metadata) to a separate **`.DLQ`** topic and commits the offset to move on. A human/automated process inspects the DLQ later. This keeps one bad message from blocking the whole partition (head-of-line blocking). 🎙️ **Script:** "Retention is cheap because I delete whole segment files by age, not individual messages, and I tier old ones to object storage. For poison messages, the consumer retries a few times with backoff, then routes the message to a dead-letter topic and moves its offset forward — otherwise one bad message blocks the entire partition behind it." ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost **Availability:** target **99.99%**. Multi-AZ replica placement (replicas of a partition in 3 different AZs) so an AZ loss still leaves a quorum. | Failure | Behavior | |---|---| | Leader broker dies | Controller promotes an ISR follower; producers refresh metadata and retry; sub-second to seconds, no committed-data loss (RPO≈0). | | Follower dies | Dropped from ISR; writes continue if ISR ≥ min.insync; rejoins & catches up later. | | Controller dies | Raft quorum elects a new controller; data plane keeps serving with cached metadata. | | ISR < min.insync.replicas | Partition **rejects writes** (CP choice) until replicas recover — alarmed loudly. | | Disk full / corruption | Segment checksums detect corruption; replica refetches; capacity alarms before full. | | Slow consumer | Pull model = natural backpressure; lag metric alerts; data retained until retention limit. | **RPO/RTO:** acked+committed data RPO≈0; RTO = failover time (seconds). Offsets are durable. **DR:** for region loss, async mirror to a second region (MirrorMaker-style) — that path is RPO>0 (replication lag) and I'd state that explicitly. **Cost (rough monthly):** dominant driver is **storage + cross-AZ network**, not compute. ~1.9 PB replicated on SSD is the big line; tiering 7-day cold data to object storage cuts it sharply. ~25 brokers + cross-AZ replication traffic (3 GB/s) — cross-AZ data transfer fees often rival the instance bill. I'd feature **cross-AZ egress** as the surprise cost and consider rack/AZ-aware fetching to trim it. **Ops:** SLO burn-rate alerts on produce/fetch p99, **consumer lag** (the #1 health signal), under-replicated partition count, and ISR shrink events; distributed tracing via message headers; runbooks for leader rebalance and disk pressure. 🎙️ **Script:** "Replicas live in three AZs, so an AZ outage still leaves a quorum and we fail over a leader in seconds with zero committed-data loss. The deliberate CP choice is that if too many replicas fall behind, we stop accepting writes rather than risk loss — and that fires a loud alarm. My top health metric is consumer lag. On cost, the surprise is cross-AZ replication traffic and SSD storage for two petabytes — so I tier cold data to object storage and use AZ-aware reads." ## 10. Trade-off Ledger | Decision | Gave up | What would reverse it | |---|---|---| | **Per-partition ordering, not global** | Total ordering across a topic | If a use case truly needs global order at low volume → single partition | | **CP on write (reject when ISR < min)** | Write availability during replica loss | If the data is loss-tolerant (metrics) → `acks=1`/AP | | **At-least-once default, exactly-once opt-in** | Zero-duplicate guarantee by default | If duplicates are catastrophic *and* throughput allows → enable transactions everywhere | | **Pull-based consumers** | Lowest possible push latency | Ultra-low-latency push use cases → add a push/streaming layer | | **Local SSD + tiering** | Operational simplicity of pure object storage | If latency tolerance loosens → object-storage-native (like WarpStream) to kill cross-AZ cost | 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd flag as deliberate: per-partition instead of global ordering, which I'd only reverse for a low-volume total-order need; choosing consistency over availability on writes, which I'd flip for loss-tolerant data; and defaulting to at-least-once, turning on exactly-once only where duplicates are truly unacceptable. Each ties straight back to my opening SLOs." ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers **1. Why partitions instead of one log per topic?** A single log means one leader, one machine, ~10 MB/s — a hard throughput ceiling and a single point of failure. Partitions let us spread a topic across many brokers for linear scale, at the cost of only-per-partition ordering. It's the central trade that makes the system scalable. > 💬 "One log caps you at one machine; partitions are how you scale past a gigabyte a second — you just give up global ordering." **2. What exactly is the high-watermark and why does it matter?** It's the highest offset that all in-sync replicas have persisted; consumers can only read up to it. This prevents a consumer from reading a message that exists only on the leader and would vanish if the leader crashed before replication. It's the line between "written" and "safe to expose." > 💬 "Consumers only see data that's already on every in-sync replica — so a crash can never make a message they read disappear." **3. How does failover avoid data loss?** Only ISR members — replicas fully caught up — are eligible to become leader, and we only ack after ISR has the data. So any committed message is on the new leader. If we allowed an out-of-sync replica to take over (unclean election), we'd trade availability for potential loss — configurable, but off by default. > 💬 "We only promote a replica that already has all the committed data, so failover is lossless by construction." **4. Why pull-based consumers instead of push?** Pull gives natural backpressure — a slow consumer just fetches slower, the broker never overwhelms it — and lets multiple consumer groups read the same retained log independently at their own pace. Push needs flow-control machinery and risks overwhelming slow consumers. The cost is slightly higher latency, mitigated by long-poll fetches. > 💬 "Pull means a slow consumer can't be overwhelmed — it just reads slower — and many groups can replay the same log independently." **5. How do consumer groups rebalance when a member dies?** Members heartbeat to a group coordinator; if one misses heartbeats, the coordinator triggers a rebalance and reassigns its partitions to surviving members. Modern designs use **incremental/cooperative rebalancing** so only affected partitions move, avoiding a full stop-the-world. The new owner resumes from the last committed offset. > 💬 "A dead consumer's partitions get reassigned to its peers, and they pick up from the last committed offset — with cooperative rebalancing so we don't pause everything." **6. What happens to ordering when a producer retries?** With an idempotent producer, each record has a sequence number per partition; the broker rejects out-of-order or duplicate sequences, so a retry can't create a gap, duplicate, or reorder. Without idempotence, you must limit max in-flight requests to 1 to preserve order, or accept possible reordering on retry. > 💬 "The sequence number lets the broker reject duplicate retries and keep order — without it you'd have to send one batch at a time." **7. How do you handle a poison/un-processable message?** The consumer retries with backoff a bounded number of times, then routes the message plus failure context to a dead-letter topic and commits its offset to move past it. This prevents head-of-line blocking where one bad message stalls the whole partition. The DLQ is inspected and reprocessed out-of-band. > 💬 "Retry a few times, then shove it into a dead-letter topic and move on, so one bad message doesn't block the partition behind it." **8. How is exactly-once actually achieved end-to-end?** Idempotent producer (sequence-number dedup) plus transactions that atomically write output records and commit the consumed input offset, with downstream consumers reading committed-only. End-to-end correctness still needs the final side effect (e.g. a DB write) to be idempotent or part of the transaction. So "exactly-once" is a property of the whole pipeline, not just the broker. > 💬 "Idempotent producer plus transactional commit of output-and-offset together — but the final side effect still has to be idempotent, or you've just moved the problem." **9. Why Raft/KRaft for the controller instead of ZooKeeper?** Metadata — who leads each partition, broker membership — must be strongly consistent and survive failures, which is exactly what a Raft quorum gives. Folding it into the brokers (KRaft) removes a separate ZooKeeper system, simplifies ops, and makes metadata propagation faster and more scalable to millions of partitions. It's one consensus log instead of two coordinating systems. > 💬 "Metadata needs consensus, and Raft gives that; baking it into the brokers kills the separate ZooKeeper dependency and scales to way more partitions." **10. How do you scale storage to petabytes affordably?** Tiered storage: hot recent segments stay on local SSD for low-latency reads, while older segments offload to object storage and are fetched on demand. This decouples retention from broker disk size — you can keep months of data without growing the cluster. The trade-off is higher latency for historical reads, which is usually fine. > 💬 "Keep recent data on SSD, age the rest into object storage — now retention isn't limited by broker disk, and old reads are just a bit slower." **11. A consumer is lagging badly — what do you do?** First confirm via the lag metric (log-end-offset minus committed offset). Add consumers up to the partition count to parallelize, or increase partition count for future scaling; check for a poison message or slow downstream. The retained log means we don't lose data while catching up, as long as we stay within retention. > 💬 "Lag is my key signal — I add consumers up to the partition count and hunt for a slow downstream or a poison message; the log holds the backlog safely until retention." **12. How do you prevent duplicate processing in at-least-once?** Make consumers idempotent: dedup by a natural message id or store a processed-offset transactionally with the side effect. Alternatively use the transactional/exactly-once path. The point is at-least-once *will* produce duplicates on retry, so the consumer must be designed to tolerate them. > 💬 "Assume duplicates will happen and dedup by message id on the consumer side — at-least-once guarantees you'll see some replays." **13. What's the single biggest bottleneck at 1M msgs/sec?** Per-partition leader throughput plus cross-AZ replication bandwidth. The leader does sequential disk writes (fast) but is capped ~10 MB/s with replication, so partition count is the lever; network for 3x replication across AZs is the other. We relieve both by spreading ~100 partitions over ~25 brokers and balancing leadership. > 💬 "It's per-partition leader throughput and replication bandwidth — I fix it with about a hundred partitions across twenty-five brokers and balanced leadership." **14. RabbitMQ vs Kafka — when would you pick the other model?** If you need per-message routing, complex routing topologies, priority queues, or delete-on-ack work distribution with low retention, a broker like RabbitMQ fits better. Kafka's log model wins for high-throughput streaming, replay, and multiple independent consumers. I chose the log model because it generalizes — a work queue is just a single consumer group that commits-then-deletes. > 💬 "For rich routing and competing-consumer work queues, RabbitMQ; for high-throughput replayable streams with many readers, the Kafka log — which I picked because it generalizes to both." 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "We're building a Kafka-style distributed log. Producers hash a message key to one of about a hundred partitions and send to that partition's leader broker, which appends to a sequential on-disk log. Each partition is replicated three ways across AZs, and a message is only acked and made visible once all in-sync replicas have it — that's our zero-loss durability point, enforced by the high-watermark consumers read up to. Ordering is guaranteed per partition because each has a single leader; we trade away global ordering for a gigabyte-a-second of throughput. Consumers pull at their own pace, track durable offsets, and pick a delivery guarantee — at-most, at-least, or exactly-once via idempotent producers and transactions. A Raft-based controller handles leadership and failover in seconds, old segments tier to object storage for cheap retention, and poison messages go to a dead-letter topic. The deliberate stance is consistency over availability on writes: if replication can't keep up, we reject rather than risk loss."

▸
View problem statement & approach
Problem statement
Design a scalable and fault-tolerant messaging system for communication between services, similar to Kafka or RabbitMQ:

Requirements:
Core Functionalities:

Publishers: Services that publish messages to topics or queues.
Subscribers: Services that consume messages from topics or queues.
Messaging Broker: A distributed system that ensures reliable message delivery and decouples publishers from subscribers.
Key Features:

Durability: Messages should persist in case of broker failure.
Scalability: Handle high throughput of messages across multiple publishers and subscribers.
Fault Tolerance: Ensure no message loss and guaranteed delivery even in case of node failures.
Ordering: Maintain message order within topics or partitions.
Delivery Semantics: Support at-least-once, at-most-once, and exactly-once delivery modes.
Extension:

Retention policies (e.g., message expiry).
Handling dead-letter queues for unprocessed messages.

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — durability, ordering, and exactly-once — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension here: we want a **firehose of throughput** (millions of messages/sec) AND **zero message loss** AND **ordering** AND **flexible delivery guarantees** — and those pull against each other. Durability and ordering want us to write everything to one ordered log on disk; throughput wants us to spread writes across many machines. The whole design is about resolving that with **partitioning + replication**.

🏢 **Company x Level lens:** I'll target a **Google-style distributed-systems bar** — I'll go deepest on one hard problem (the replication/consistency core) and name rejected alternatives. Tell me if this is Amazon (I'll make cost & ops first-class) or a startup (I'll start with a managed broker) and I'll re-tilt. This is a **senior** round so I'll build one coherent system with real depth rather than an org-wide platform.

**Functional (core flows — "above the line"):**
- **Publishers can publish a message to a named topic** (optionally to a specific partition via a key), and get an ack.
- **Subscribers can consume messages from a topic**, in order within a partition, tracking their position (offset).
- **The broker durably stores and replicates messages** so nothing is lost when a node dies, and delivers them reliably.
- **Support configurable delivery semantics** — at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once.
- **Consumer groups** — multiple consumers share a topic's partitions so we can scale out consumption.

**Below the line (out of scope for now):** retention policies & TTL expiry, dead-letter queues, schema registry, multi-tenancy/quotas, geo-replication, ACL/auth admin UI. *(I'll touch retention + DLQ in deep dives as extensions.)*

**Non-Functional (the system should…):**
- **Be highly available** for both publish and consume — target **99.99%** (≈ 52 min/yr down); a node failure must not lose acked data.
- **Be durable** — an acked message survives f node failures (replication factor giving us that). **RPO ≈ 0** for acked, fully-replicated writes.
- **Be high throughput / low latency** — handle **~1M msgs/sec** sustained; **p99 publish ack < 10ms** (in-region), consume add-latency single-digit ms.
- **Preserve ordering within a partition** (not globally — that's the deliberate trade-off).
- **Scale horizontally** — add brokers/partitions to grow linearly.
- **Consistency:** strong/linearizable for the *committed offset* of a partition (one source of truth per partition); consumer offset commits are durable.

> 💬 "The thing I want to call out up front is that global ordering across a whole topic is incompatible with high throughput — so I'll give strong ordering *per partition* and make partitioning a first-class design decision."

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true **exactly-once** end-to-end is only achievable with idempotent producers + transactional consumers; I'll be precise that the broker gives exactly-once *within the system*, and end-to-end requires the consumer to participate.

🎙️ **Script:** "So we're building a distributed pub/sub log like Kafka. Publishers write messages to topics, subscribers read them, and the broker in the middle durably stores and replicates everything so we never lose an acked message even if a machine dies. The hard requirements are durability, ordering, and flexible delivery guarantees — at-most, at-least, and exactly-once. My one big up-front decision is that I'll guarantee ordering *per partition*, not globally, because global ordering would bottleneck the whole topic on a single machine. I'm targeting four nines of availability, about a million messages a second, and single-digit-millisecond publish acks."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

Questions I'd ask, and why each changes the design:
- **"What's the message size and throughput profile?"** — Small (a few KB) high-volume events vs large payloads changes batching, and whether we store payloads inline or in blob storage.
- **"Pub/sub (many independent consumers, like Kafka) or work-queue (compete for messages, like RabbitMQ)?"** — This is the biggest fork. Kafka = a *retained log* multiple groups re-read; RabbitMQ = messages *deleted on ack*. I'll design the **log model (Kafka-style)** and show how a queue is a special case.
- **"How long must messages be retained?"** — Drives storage sizing and tiering (local disk vs object storage).
- **"Which delivery semantic is the priority?"** — Most systems live on at-least-once + idempotent consumers; exactly-once is expensive.
- **"Single region or multi-region?"** — Multi-region forces a story on cross-region replication lag and which region owns writes.
- **"Ordering scope — per key, per partition, or global?"** — Confirms the per-partition trade-off.

**Assumptions I'll proceed with:** Kafka-style **retained, partitioned, replicated log**; ~1M msgs/sec, avg 1KB; single region, multi-AZ; 7-day retention; at-least-once is the default with exactly-once as an opt-in; ordering is per-partition.

🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to design the Kafka-style retained-log model rather than a delete-on-ack work queue, since it generalizes to both. Are you good with that, or do you specifically want RabbitMQ-style competing-consumer queues?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll defer full estimation and only compute the few numbers that drive decisions — throughput, storage footprint, and partition count.
